response:
The recent decline in German housing construction starts was driven by a combination of weak demand, high project cancellation rates, insufficient government support, and sharply rising timber costs.

According to the ifo Business Survey published on March 16, 2026, sentiment in German residential construction improved slightly in early 2026, but the sector remains under pressure. The share of companies reporting too few orders was still high at 48.0% in February, only marginally down from 49.8% in January. At the same time, project cancellations increased, with 11.3% of firms reporting cancellations in February, up from 11.1% the previous month. The survey notes that despite a more positive outlook, there is little sign of a sustained upturn in order books.

Data from Holz-Zentralblatt highlights a severe contraction in the core market for prefabricated wood houses. In the past year, building permits for this segment fell by 38.1%, closely mirroring a 40.4% decline in the overall segment. The article attributes this sharp downturn not only to market forces but also to what it describes as "undecided and insufficient" government support for homebuilders and the construction industry, with calls for more effective policy intervention to stimulate housing construction.

On the supply side, German timber prices have risen sharply in early 2026, with forest owners and sawmills reporting record-high revenues for coniferous roundwood and acute raw material shortages. Sawmills face empty warehouses and escalating costs, as described in a January 21, 2026 report, which points to an extremely tight market supply. This has further increased the cost pressures on housing construction projects.

Muted market activity for sawn timber and weak domestic construction demand were also observed in early 2026, according to a February 2026 market outlook. German buyers have focused on essential procurement only, as sawlog cost pressures persist and inventory building remains limited.

The combination of persistently weak demand, a high rate of project cancellations, insufficient government support, and sharply rising timber prices directly contributed to the recent decline in German housing construction starts.
reference: Sentiment in German residential construction improved slightly in early 2026, but 48.0% of companies still reported too few orders in February, down only marginally from 49.8% in January. Project cancellations rose from 11.1% to 11.3% of firms in February according to the ifo Business Survey. Building permits for prefabricated wood houses fell 38.1% in 2023, mirroring a 40.4% decline in the overall single and two-family home segment. Rising construction interest rates suppressed investment willingness and the Ukraine war drove up energy costs and building material prices. Government support for homebuilders was described as undecided and insufficient by BDF. German timber prices reached record highs in early 2026 with spruce B 2b+ averaging €129–132 per cubic meter, sawmills facing empty warehouses and an acute raw material shortage.

user_input: What triggered the drop in German building permit approvals in 2025?
retrieved_contexts: ['The construction industry in Germany declined in real terms by 3% in 2025, owing to falling building permits and rising construction material costs.\n\nAccording to Destatis, the total number of non-residential building permits approved fell by 3.6% year-on-year (YoY) in the first nine months of 2025, following an overall annual fall of 5.8% in 2024. Similarly, the construction production index declined by 2.7% YoY in the first nine months of 2025, following an overall annual fall of 3.4% in 2024, according to the Eurostat.', 'Germany’s construction industry has weathered a severe cri-sis. Between 2022 and 2024, interest rate hikes and soaring construction costs caused residential construction to plum-met. Weak overall economic performance further dampened construction activity. The bottom seems to have been reached in 2025.']
response:
Headline Finding — The drop in German building permit approvals in 2025 was triggered by interest rate hikes, soaring construction costs, and weak economic performance, according to DIW Berlin and Destatis data.

DIW Berlin reports that between 2022 and 2024, residential construction plummeted as interest rate increases and rising construction costs made financing difficult, leading many households to defer building projects. This trend continued into 2025, with real construction output declining again, though the contraction was smaller than in previous years. Destatis data confirms that non-residential building permits fell by 3.6% year-on-year in the first nine months of 2025, following a 5.8% annual drop in 2024.
reference: Between 2022 and 2024, interest rate hikes and soaring construction costs caused residential construction in Germany to plummet, as financing became difficult and households deferred building projects. Weak overall economic performance further dampened construction activity. Real construction output declined again in 2025, though the contraction was significantly smaller than in prior years. According to Destatis, non-residential building permits fell 3.6% year-on-year in the first nine months of 2025, following an overall annual decline of 5.8% in 2024. The construction production index declined 2.7% year-on-year in the first nine months of 2025 according to Eurostat.

response:
**Headline Finding** — Germany approved 19,500 housing units in January 2026, an 8.4% increase over January 2025.

According to the Statistisches Bundesamt (Destatis), 19,500 housing units were approved across Germany in January 2026, which is 1,500 more units than in January 2025, marking an 8.4% year-on-year increase. Of these, 16,400 units were new builds, up 7.4% or 1,100 units from the previous year. Housing created through the conversion or extension of existing buildings rose by 13.5% to 3,100 units.

Within the new build segment, 16,000 units were approved in newly constructed residential buildings, a 6.0% increase (+900 units) compared to January 2025. Approvals for single-family houses rose by 12.6% to 3,800 units, while two-family houses saw a 26.1% jump to 1,200 units. The largest share was in multi-family houses, with 10,500 units approved, representing a 7.1% increase (+700 units) over the previous year. In contrast, approvals for residential homes (Wohnheime) fell sharply by 47.6% to 500 units.

Industry commentary from the Zentrale Immobilien Ausschuss (ZIA) notes a cautious optimism, highlighting that the upward trend in multi-family housing approvals seen since 2025 appears to be continuing into 2026, but warns against excessive expectations for a rapid recovery.

19,500 housing units approved in January 2026 — the highest January figure since 2022 — signals a modest but clear rebound in German residential construction approvals.
reference: Germany approved 19,500 housing units in January 2026, an 8.4% increase compared to January 2025. Of these, 16,400 were new builds, up 7.4% year-on-year. Housing created through conversion or extension of existing buildings rose 13.5% to 3,100 units. Multi-family housing approvals reached 10,500 units, up 7.1%.

**Headline Finding** — Roundwood supply in Germany remains exceptionally tight in early 2026, driving record price increases for spruce and pine and leaving sawmill inventories depleted.

The German timber market entered 2026 with acute supply shortages, particularly for spruce and pine roundwood. According to multiple sources, available volumes are insufficient to fully meet sawmill demand, resulting in empty warehouses and what the German Federal Association of the Wood Industry describes as an acute “raw material shortage.” Sawmills are facing skyrocketing costs, and forest farmers are receiving higher revenues for coniferous roundwood than ever before. Price increases for the key grade Fichte B 2b+ reached €5–10 per Festmeter in early 2026, with sawmill gate prices reportedly €25–30 higher than the previous year.

Several factors are driving this tight supply. First, sources confirm that the shortage is not demand-driven; rather, it is a result of constrained supply. The German Timber Industry Association (HDH) reports a rapid deterioration in raw timber availability and highlights regulatory impacts, specifically referencing the Restoration Ordinance (WVO) and its prohibition on degradation, which is affecting sustainable forest management and limiting harvest volumes. Additionally, the ongoing scarcity is exacerbated by a cold winter, which has increased heating demand and drawn more wood into energy markets, further tightening roundwood availability for sawmills.

External factors are also contributing to the situation. Ukraine’s extension of roundwood and fuelwood export restrictions through 2026 under a zero-quota licensing system has reduced regional supply flexibility, increasing competition for available timber within Germany. This has forced mills to rely more heavily on domestic sources, where logging remains subdued and raw material shortages are limiting output.

Spruce and pine roundwood prices reached new records in Q1 2026, with industrial wood and energy wood prices also rising sharply as a result of the overall shortage. Sawmills are moving shipments quickly to maintain operations, but many report that current contracts only run through the end of April, intensifying the urgency to secure new supply.

Spruce B 2b+ price increases of €5–10/Fm and sawmill gate prices up €25–30 year-on-year as of Q1 2026 make immediate procurement and contract renegotiation the top operational priority.
reference: Roundwood supply in Germany is exceptionally tight in early 2026 due to insufficient harvest volumes, reduced salvage wood from bark beetle and storm damage, and strong competing demand from firewood and industrial wood markets. The German Timber Industry Association (HDH) describes the situation as a raw material shortage. Spruce and pine roundwood supply is insufficient to meet sawmill demand in many regions, leading to empty warehouses and record price increases in Q1 2026.

response:
German sawmill regions most affected by weak construction demand in Q1 2026 are those supplying residential and road construction, with order declines of 15.5% and 18.7% respectively in January.

The Statistische Bundesamt reported that in January 2026, the German construction sector saw a real decline in orders of 4.3% compared to January 2025, and a 5.1% drop from December 2025. The most severe contractions were in road construction, with orders down 18.7% year-on-year, and in residential construction, where orders fell by 15.5%. These segments are major consumers of structural timber and engineered wood products, directly impacting sawmill demand in regions focused on supplying these markets.

Industry commentary from BAUINDUSTRIE President Peter Hübner, published in January 2026, confirms that although the sector expects a modest real growth of 2.5% for the year, the recovery is uneven. Residential construction is projected to recover only slowly, with completion numbers expected to continue declining in the near term. Order declines of 15.5% in residential and 18.7% in road construction in January 2026 directly reduced timber demand for sawmills in regions supplying these sectors.
reference: In January 2026, the German construction sector recorded a real order decline of 4.3% compared to January 2025 and 5.1% compared to December 2025. Road construction saw the steepest drop at 18.7% year-on-year, followed by residential construction at 15.5%, and commercial construction at 2.0%. Revenue fell 7.5% in real terms, partly due to poor weather conditions. BAUINDUSTRIE forecasts 2.5% real growth for the sector in 2026, with residential construction recovering only modestly at 2.0% and completion numbers continuing to decline. Sawmills supplying residential and road construction segments were therefore most exposed to weak demand in Q1 2026.

response:
**Headline Finding** — The most challenging compliance requirement for German timber exporters under the EU Deforestation Regulation in early 2026 is the obligation to collect and validate geolocation data and achieve full supply chain traceability, despite the postponement of main deadlines to December 2026.

Regulation (EU) 2025/2650, published in December 2025, postponed the main EUDR compliance deadline for large operators to 30 December 2026, and for micro and small enterprises to 30 June 2027. However, sources confirm that the core compliance obligations remain unchanged, and German timber exporters are required to prepare for operationally demanding requirements. These include collecting and validating supplier information, capturing geolocation data for relevant forest-risk commodities, and standardizing risk assessment methodologies.

Industry reporting highlights that the most difficult aspect is building end-to-end supply chain traceability and integrating geolocation data capture into existing procurement and compliance systems. This process is particularly complex for exporters with global, multi-tier supply chains, as it requires coordination across procurement, sustainability, compliance, and IT departments. The EUDR also mandates segregation of compliant and non-compliant products at every supply chain junction and assessment of the deforestation-free status of each plot of land.

While the April 2026 Simplification Package is expected to clarify some compliance issues, sources emphasize that it will not alter the fundamental requirements, including the legality and traceability obligations. As a result, German timber exporters must continue implementing full EUDR compliance protocols without delay, focusing on the most technically demanding elements—geolocation data collection and supply chain traceability.

Roundwood exporters face immediate operational challenges in integrating geolocation and traceability systems ahead of the December 2026 deadline, making IT and compliance workflow upgrades the top priority.
reference: The most challenging compliance requirement under the EUDR for German timber exporters is geolocation data collection and full supply chain traceability. Despite the postponement of main deadlines to 30 December 2026 for large operators under Regulation EU 2025/2650, businesses must still build capabilities for supplier data collection, geolocation capture, risk assessment, documentation, and submission workflows. The European Commission must present a simplification report by 30 April 2026.

**Headline Finding** — Spruce roundwood prices in Germany surged to record highs in the first two months of 2026 due to acute supply shortages and heightened winter demand.

At the start of 2026, German sawmills faced an exceptionally tight supply of spruce roundwood, with available volumes in many regions insufficient to meet demand. According to the Hauptverband der Deutschen Holzindustrie (HDH), the raw wood supply situation deteriorated rapidly, prompting industry warnings of an acute wood shortage. This scarcity led to significant price increases for spruce B 2b+, with net prices in early January averaging between €129 and €132 per solid cubic meter, and in some cases even higher. Compared to the previous year, this represents a rise of approximately €25–30 per cubic meter paid to forest owners.

The price rally was further intensified by a cold and snowy winter, which accelerated inventory drawdowns and sharply increased demand for energy wood, including firewood and industrial assortments. Forest owner associations reported that additional premiums were sometimes paid for fresh industrial wood in the first quarter, and the sawmill industry raised prices across all coniferous roundwood grades. The acute shortage also benefited lower-quality wood, with strong demand extending beyond sawlogs to energy and industrial wood assortments.

Sawmills, facing empty warehouses and escalating procurement costs, responded by raising prices significantly in the first quarter of 2026. The HDH also cited concerns about the potential impact of the Wiederherstellungsverordnung (WVO) on sustainable forest management, though the immediate price drivers were the supply-demand imbalance and weather-driven consumption surge.

Spruce B 2b+ at €129–132/Fm as of January 2026 — up €25–30/Fm year-on-year — makes securing supply contracts before further price escalation the immediate priority.
reference: Spruce roundwood prices in Germany reached record highs in the first two months of 2026 due to acute supply shortages. The leading spruce B 2b+ grade averaged 129 to 132 euros per solid cubic meter in January 2026 with prices in some cases exceeding 135 euros. Pine roundwood averaged 95 to 98 euros per cubic meter. Forest owners received approximately 25 to 30 euros more per cubic meter than the previous year. The cold winter increased firewood demand competing with sawmill roundwood supply.

response:
**Headline Finding** — German forest owners are struggling to meet sawmill roundwood demand in early 2026 despite record prices due to a sharp drop in damaged wood supply and intensified competition from firewood and industrial wood markets.

Sawmills across Germany are offering record prices for roundwood in Q1 2026, with spruce B 2b+ fetching €130–133 per cubic meter and occasionally up to €135, while B/C grades are at €96–100 per cubic meter. Pine wood prices have also surged, reaching €95–98 per cubic meter for the 2b+ assortment in grade B/C. These figures represent an increase of €25–30 per cubic meter compared to the previous year, according to forest owner reports from January and March 2026.

Despite these high prices, sawmill warehouses remain empty in many regions because fresh wood deliveries are extremely limited. The German Federal Association of the Wood Industry describes the situation as a "raw material shortage," warning of an impending "existential crisis" if supply does not improve. The primary cause is a dramatic reduction in the availability of damaged wood, which historically made up about half of all timber harvested in Germany. The Bavarian State Institute for Forestry and Forest Management confirms that this decline in damaged wood supply has severely constrained overall market availability.

Compounding the shortage, the cold and snowy winter of early 2026 has sharply increased demand for firewood and energy wood, accelerating inventory reduction. Forest owner associations report that shipments of industrial wood have been postponed to prioritize roundwood deliveries to sawmills, but the surge in firewood demand—driven by an intense heating season—has diverted significant volumes away from sawmills. Additional price premiums for fresh industrial wood in Q1 have further incentivized selective harvesting for non-sawmill uses.

Spruce B 2b+ at €130–135/Fm in March 2026 — a record price level — has not been sufficient to overcome the combined impact of reduced damaged wood supply and heightened firewood competition, directly limiting forest owners’ ability to meet sawmill roundwood demand.
reference: German forest owners are struggling to meet sawmill roundwood demand despite record prices because the cold winter increased firewood and industrial wood demand competing with sawmill deliveries. The extreme shortage was caused by low supply of salvage wood from bark beetle damage and storms which normally accounts for about half of harvested timber. Spruce B 2b+ prices reached 130 to 135 euros per cubic meter. The HDH warned of an existential crisis for the sawmill industry if more wood does not come to market.

response:
**Headline Finding** — No evidence confirms that rising electricity or gas costs have negatively affected German sawmill operating margins in Q1 2026; instead, official sources report energy cost relief measures for industry this year.

Reporting from the Bundesministerium für Wirtschaft und Energie (BMWi) confirms that, as of 2026, the German government has implemented a permanent reduction in the electricity tax for the manufacturing sector and abolished the gas storage levy. These measures are explicitly intended to lower energy costs for over 600,000 companies, including those in forestry and sawmilling. The BMWi states that the removal of the gas storage levy will "significantly" reduce gas prices and have a dampening effect on electricity prices, benefiting energy-intensive industries.

No sources reviewed report an increase in electricity or gas costs for sawmills in Q1 2026. Instead, the available evidence points to energy prices easing due to these policy interventions. While sawmills are facing sharply higher roundwood input costs—such as Fichte B 2b+ rising to €129–132/Fm in January 2026, up €25–30/Fm year-on-year—there is no indication that energy costs have contributed to margin pressure in the current quarter.

BMWi energy tax reductions and the abolition of the gas storage levy in 2026 are confirmed to have lowered energy costs for German sawmills, so rising electricity and gas prices have not negatively affected sawmill operating margins in Q1 2026.
reference: No direct evidence confirms that rising electricity or gas costs negatively impacted German sawmill operating margins in Q1 2026. The German government implemented a permanent reduction in the electricity tax for the manufacturing sector and abolished the gas storage levy from 2026. These measures reduced electricity costs for over 600,000 industrial companies. Sawmill operating pressure in Q1 2026 was driven primarily by record high roundwood input costs not energy costs.

**Headline Finding** — Timber prices in Germany continued to rise sharply in February 2026, with both roundwood and firewood reaching new highs across multiple assortments.

In February 2026, German sawn softwood roundwood prices remained at record levels. According to a market report published on 18 February 2026, the national average price for pine (Kiefer, classes A, B, C, 2a+) reached €90 per solid cubic meter, with a monthly increase of approximately €2. The same report notes that pine logs in some regions, such as Lower Austria, were sold for up to €100 per cubic meter. This upward movement is attributed to strong demand, particularly for spruce, which has also driven up pine prices. Earlier reporting from January 2026 confirms that spruce B 2b+ net prices averaged between €129 and €132 per solid cubic meter, while pine wood in the B/C grade fetched €95 to €98 per cubic meter, representing a year-on-year increase of €25–30 for forest owners.

Firewood prices also climbed in February 2026. For birch and the popular beech/birch mix, prices mostly ranged between €105 and €110 per Schüttraummeter, with some DIY stores charging up to €160. Oven-ready beech firewood (30–33 cm) was typically priced between €115 and €125 per Schüttraummeter, with some offers reaching €145 and DIY stores asking around €190. Fresh, non-dried beech averaged €95–105 per Schüttraummeter, occasionally exceeding €110. For coniferous firewood (spruce and pine), pre-dried material was available from timber dealers at €65–75 per Schüttraummeter, while oven-ready coniferous firewood was priced at €85–95, both with a rising trend.

Sources consistently attribute these price increases to a combination of strong demand and limited supply, with some regions experiencing logistical challenges such as thaw-related transport restrictions that further constrain the market.

Spruce B 2b+ at €129–132/Fm and pine at €90–100/Fm as of February 2026 — both up sharply year-on-year — make early procurement and contract negotiation the immediate priority.
reference: German timber prices continued rising sharply in February 2026. The national average price for pine logs reached 90 euros per solid cubic meter in February up approximately 2 euros month-on-month. Firewood prices for beech reached 115 to 125 euros per cubic meter from professional dealers. Softwood firewood cost 65 to 75 euros per cubic meter pre-dried. Spruce B 2b+ roundwood prices remained at record levels. Some regions saw supply exceeding local sawmill capacity due to thaw restrictions on transport.

response:
German housing permits rose 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026, marking the first significant increase after three years of decline.

According to Fordaq, Germany issued 19,500 housing permits in January 2026, representing an 8.4% increase compared to January 2025. This marks a notable turnaround following a prolonged slump, as confirmed by multiple sources referencing the broader 2025 rebound. The Federal Statistical Office reported that 238,500 residential permits were granted in 2025, a 10.8% rise from the 215,300 permits in 2024, which had been the lowest level since 2010.

Industry commentary from the German Macroeconomic Policy Institute (IMK) and Minister of Construction Verena Hubertz described the permit increase as a strong signal of recovery in the housing sector. The improvement is attributed to efforts to reduce bureaucracy and the introduction of subsidy programmes, which are expected to help sustain the positive trend into 2026.

January’s 8.4% year-on-year increase in permits to 19,500 units signals a clear upward trend, making monitoring monthly permit releases the immediate priority for tracking construction sector momentum.

In full-year 2025, the Federal Statistical Office recorded 238,500 residential permits, a 10.8% increase from 215,300 in 2024, which had been the lowest level since 2010. New-build apartment approvals within that figure rose 12.6% to 198,100 units, while conversions increased 2.7% to 40,400. The German Macroeconomic Policy Institute described the permit recovery as a strong signal of a sector turnaround. Reduced bureaucracy and new subsidy programmes are expected to sustain the positive trend into 2026.

Obviously higher duties and tariffs remain a core theme driving wood product markets since the beginning of 2025. response:
**Headline Finding** — US announcements of new lumber tariffs in January 2026 triggered immediate concern among European timber trade bodies about retaliatory measures and the risk of a transatlantic trade conflict affecting wood product flows.

According to the Gesamtverband Deutscher Holzhandel (GD Holz), the European Timber Trade Federation (ETTF) responded to US tariff announcements by issuing a joint position paper to EU Commission President von der Leyen and Trade Commissioner Šefčovič. The ETTF warned that including wood products in retaliatory tariffs could provoke the US to impose tariffs on EU wood product exports, potentially escalating a trade war in the timber sector. As of the latest reporting, US imports of wood products from the EU remain exempt from tariffs, but the White House is conducting a separate investigation into these imports.

The EU Commission confirmed that, as of May 16, 2025, it intends to include American wood products in its list of retaliatory tariffs against the United States. This move follows earlier efforts by European industry groups to avoid the inclusion of wood products in such measures, due to fears of reciprocal US action.

Fastmarkets reports that, while wood panels were not included in the October Section 232 tariffs, dimensional lumber, logs, and some value-added products such as kitchen cabinets were affected. The report also notes that, since early 2025, high duties and tariffs have been a cenD12tral theme in wood product markets, with record duties influencing trade flows and market sentiment.

Available evidence confirms that the US tariff announcements in January 2026 heightened the risk of a trade conflict, prompting immediate lobbying and policy responses from European timber organizations and the EU Commission, but no direct price or volume impacts on European timber markets have yet been documented in the sources.

reference: US tariff announcements in January 2026 triggered concern among European timber trade bodies. The European Timber Trade Federation issued a position paper to EU Commission President von der Leyen warning against including wood products in retaliatory tariff measures. As of late March 2026 no active US tariffs on European wood imports were in place but an official investigation into European wood imports impact on US national security was initiated. Short-term US softwood lumber prices spiked to COVID-pandemic levels following the tariff announcements.

Bundesagrarminister Alois Rainer stated: \'We must balance effective global forest protection with the competitiveness of our companies.\' The proposals include regional collective reports for small primary producers, simplified proof requirements for imports from low-risk countries, limited legality checks, centralized Commission responsibility for uniform implementation, and reduced burdens on supervisory authorities."', 'The German proposals include regional collective declarations instead of individual farm-level reporting, simplified compliance for low-risk country imports, and restriction of checks to the point of first EU market entry. AGDW president Andreas Bitter welcomed the initiative, calling for elimination of individual reporting obligations for forest owners. Violations carry fines of up to four percent of annual turnover. Penalties also include product seizure and market exclusion."', 'Large and medium enterprises must comply with EUDR from 30 December 2026. Micro and small enterprises have until 30 June 2027. Only the first operator placing products on the EU market must submit due diligence statements — downstream operators and traders are exempt. Small primary producers in low-risk countries may submit a single simplified declaration instead of recurring obligations. Records must be retained for five years for authority review', "he Deutsche Umwelthilfe criticized the German government's simplification proposals submitted to the EU Commission on 30 March 2026, warning that weakening enforcement controls risks creating loopholes for illegal timber imports entering the EU market"]
response:
The practical application of the EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) in Germany was officially postponed by one year to 30 December 2026, with further simplifications and staggered deadlines proposed for small enterprises and private forest owners.

Sources confirm that all companies now have until 30 December 2026 to implement EUDR requirements, following a formal one-year postponement agreed by EU institutions, including the European Council and Parliament, after Germany submitted improvement proposals. For micro and small enterprises, the application of EUDR obligations is further delayed to 30 June 2027, according to Deutsche Handwerks Zeitung and Customs Support reporting.

The German government submitted additional proposals to the European Commission in late March 2026, seeking to make the EUDR more practical and less burdensome for the timber sector. These proposals include a simplified, one-time declaration for small private forest owners and a requirement that only companies placing relevant products on the EU market for the first time (such as EU forest owners and importers) must submit due diligence statements, exempting downstream traders and processors from this obligation.

Industry groups and NGOs have responded with mixed reactions. While the timber sector and small forest owners have demanded further simplifications and relief from administrative burdens, the Deutsche Umwelthilfe (DUH) criticized the government’s proposals, warning that easing controls could create loopholes for illegal timber imports.

The next phase of EUDR implementation will depend on whether the EU Commission introduces further simplifications by the end of April 2026, as requested by member states. Until then, the new deadlines and proposed relief measures are the operative framework for German timber companies.

Roundwood and timber operators should prioritize reviewing compliance strategies in light of the 30 December 2026 deadline and monitor EU Commission announcements for any additional regulatory changes before mid-year.
reference: The EU Parliament voted 405 to 242 on 17 December 2025 to postpone the EUDR by one year, with the EU Council adopting the changes the following day and publication in the Official Journal on 23 December 2025. Large and medium operators must comply from 30 December 2026, while micro and small enterprises have until 30 June 2027. Only the operator first placing a product on the EU market must submit a due diligence statement, exempting downstream traders and processors. Small private forest owners may submit a single simplified declaration. Printed products including books and newspapers are excluded from scope. The EU Commission must submit a simplification review by 30 April 2026. On 30 March 2026, the German Federal Ministry of Agriculture submitted proposals to the EU Commission including regional collective reports for small primary producers, simplified proof requirements for low-risk country imports, and restriction of legality checks to the point of first EU market entry. Bundesagrarminister Alois Rainer stated that global forest protection and competitiveness must be balanced. AGDW president Andreas Bitter called for elimination of individual reporting obligations for forest owners. Violations of the EUDR carry fines of up to four percent of annual turnover. The Deutsche Umwelthilfe criticized the proposals, warning they risk creating loopholes for illegal timber imports.

response:
**Headline Finding** — Between January and March 2026, Germany announced several major housing construction policy initiatives, including the planned founding of a federal housing construction company, the introduction of the 'Gebäudetyp-E-Gesetz', and the rollout of the 'Bau-Turbo' permitting acceleration measures.

Federal Building Minister Verena Hubertz announced plans to establish a new Bundeswohnungsbaugesellschaft (federal housing construction company) to address lagging housing construction in Germany. This initiative was reported on March 29, 2026, and is positioned as a direct response to ongoing shortfalls in new housing supply.

In March 2026, Hubertz also confirmed that the 'Gebäudetyp-E-Gesetz' is scheduled to be passed within the year. This law aims to reduce construction costs by simplifying building regulations for a specific building type, with the legislative process expected to conclude in 2026.

Additionally, the federal 'Bau-Turbo' initiative has already resulted in nearly 750 additional building permits being issued in Hamburg, according to reports from March 24, 2026. This policy is designed to accelerate the approval process for new housing projects, though its reception has been mixed.

Separately, the Building Modernization Act is set to implement the EU Energy Performance of Buildings Directive (Directive (EU) 2024/1275) by May 2026. The legislative process for this act is to be completed before July 1, 2026, and it will update energy efficiency requirements for new and existing buildings, including maintaining the 65% renewable energy requirement in municipalities with over 100,000 inhabitants until at least June 30, 2026.

Federal housing construction policy in Germany is currently undergoing significant change, with multiple new laws and initiatives announced or advanced in Q1 2026 — immediate attention should focus on the legislative progress of the Bundeswohnungsbaugesellschaft, the 'Gebäudetyp-E-Gesetz', and the Building Modernization Act as these will directly shape construction activity and regulatory requirements in the coming months.
reference: Between January and March 2026 Germany announced several major housing construction policies. Federal Building Minister Verena Hubertz announced plans to establish a new federal housing construction company. The Gebaeudetyp-E-Gesetz to reduce construction costs was announced for 2026. The Bau-Turbo permitting acceleration measure resulted in nearly 750 additional building approvals in Hamburg alone. The KfW EH55 subsidy programme for new builds was reactivated in January 2026.

response:
**Headline Finding** — Industrial energy prices in Germany fell sharply between January and March 2026, but sawmill production costs remained under pressure due to record-high timber prices and raw material shortages.

According to the German Federal Statistical Office (Destatis), energy prices for industrial producers in January 2026 were 11.8% lower than in January 2025, and down 3.2% compared to December 2025. This decline in energy costs was a major factor in the overall 3.0% year-on-year drop in producer prices for industrial goods in January 2026. Policy measures also contributed: from 2026, the German government permanently reduced the electricity tax for industrial sectors—including sawmills, forestry, and agriculture—to the EU minimum rate, and introduced an annual €6.5 billion subsidy to stabilize transmission grid fees. These changes were designed to ease the energy cost burden for approximately 600,000 companies, though the German Chamber of Commerce and Industry estimates only about 15% of firms will benefit directly from the tax cut.

Despite these reductions in energy costs, sawmills in Germany have faced escalating production costs in Q1 2026, primarily due to record-high timber prices and acute raw material shortages. Reports indicate that spruce B/C grade 2b+ timber reached €130–133 per cubic meter, with some contracts as high as €135, while lower B/C grades ranged from €96–100 per cubic meter. Sawmills are described as struggling with "exploding costs" and "acute raw material undersupply," with inventories running low and supply unable to meet demand. These pressures have outweighed any relief from lower energy prices, as the cost of procuring timber has surged to unprecedented levels.

Energy price relief measures, including the tax cut and grid fee subsidy, have reduced the energy component of sawmill production costs, but the dominant driver of overall cost increases in early 2026 has been the sharp rise in timber prices and supply constraints.

Spruce B/C grade 2b+ timber prices at €130–135/m³ in Q1 2026 have more than offset the impact of an 11.8% year-on-year drop in industrial energy prices, leaving sawmill production costs elevated despite policy-driven energy relief.
reference: Industrial energy prices in Germany fell 11.8% year-on-year in January 2026 and 3.2% month-on-month according to Destatis. The German government reduced the electricity tax for industry to the EU minimum and abolished the gas storage levy from 2026. Despite lower energy costs sawmill production costs remained under severe pressure due to record-high roundwood input prices with spruce B 2b+ at 129 to 145 euros per cubic meter. Gas prices spiked sharply in March 2026 due to the Iran conflict reaching over 54 euros per MWh on 3 March 2026.

response:
**Headline Finding** — German building permits rose 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026, signaling a nascent recovery in timber demand.

According to multiple sources, the number of building permits issued in Germany for January 2026 reached 19,500, representing an 8.4% increase compared to the previous year. This marks the first notable uptick in residential construction permits after several years of decline, with the upward trend particularly evident in both single- and multi-family housing segments. Handelsblatt and ZDF confirm that this increase in permits is providing renewed optimism for the German construction sector, which is a key driver of domestic timber demand.

The ifo Institute projects that the German construction sector will grow by 2.4% in 2026, ending a period of stagnation. This forecast is supported by a strong rise in order intake observed in November 2025, indicating that the positive momentum in building activity is expected to continue at least in the near term. Industry leaders, such as Wolfgang Schubert-Raab of the Zentralverband Deutsches Baugewerbe (ZDB), have also publicly stated that confidence is returning to the sector after years of contraction.

However, several sources caution that external risks remain. Handelsblatt and ZDF both highlight the potential impact of the Iran conflict, which could drive up energy prices and lead to interest rate hikes, potentially dampening the recovery in construction activity and, by extension, timber demand. Despite these uncertainties, the current data for Q1 2026 points to a clear reversal of the previous downward trend in building permits, which is a leading indicator for timber consumption in Germany.

Building permits rising 8.4% to 19,500 units in January 2026, alongside a 2.4% construction sector growth forecast by the ifo Institute, directly signal a recovery in German timber demand, though geopolitical and macroeconomic risks could still temper this trend.
reference: German building permits rose 8.4% year-on-year to 19,500 units in January 2026 signaling a nascent timber demand recovery. The ifo Institute forecasts German construction volume to grow 2.4% in 2026 after stagnation. However the construction recovery has not yet translated into decisive increases in roundwood purchasing at sawmills as supply tightness and record prices are driven by supply constraints rather than demand growth. Risks to the recovery include potential interest rate increases due to the Iran conflict.

response:
Headline Finding — US tariff announcements and legal disputes between January and March 2026 have created volatility in international lumber markets, with German sawn timber prices edging up and supply conditions tightening amid ongoing trade policy uncertainty.

Between January and March 2026, the US government, under President Donald Trump, announced additional tariffs on EU imports, including wood products, exceeding the previously agreed 15% cap. On 20 February 2026, the US Supreme Court ruled some of these tariffs unlawful, but President Trump responded by announcing a further 10% tariff, with public calls for a 15% surcharge. According to KPMG, from 1 May 2026, a compensation mechanism equal to 2.5% of the MSRP of US production is set to apply, corresponding to a hypothetical 25% tariff on parts representing 10% of value.

German industry associations, including GD Holz, confirmed that US wood products are included in the EU’s planned retaliatory tariff measures, with a 25% tariff on a range of US wood imports to the EU scheduled from 16 May 2025, pending official publication in the EU Official Journal. This ongoing tariff escalation has heightened uncertainty for German importers and timber processors, as confirmed by GD Holz and ETTF communications with relevant German ministries.

Market data from January 2026 shows that German spruce sawfalling 44-50×150 mm prices increased by 3.17% compared to December, according to the WFFC2026 workshop summary. This price movement is attributed to a rise in international demand and supply chain adjustments following the loss of Russian and Belarusian timber since 2022, with Finland increasing its market share in several European destinations. Reporting from April 2026 further highlights that price fluctuations in the US lumber market are creating opportunities and challenges for European producers, including those in Germany.

Spruce sawfalling 44-50×150 mm in Germany rose by 3.17% month-on-month in January 2026 — a movement sources trace to increased international demand and tariff-driven supply chain disruptions affecting both US and EU timber flows.
reference: On 20 February 2026, the US Supreme Court ruled some US tariffs unlawful, following which President Trump announced an additional 10% tariff. The EU confirmed that wood products are included in retaliatory tariff measures of 25% on US imports planned from 16 May 2025. GD Holz and ETTF submitted a position paper to EU Commission President von der Leyen opposing inclusion of timber in retaliatory measures, warning that the US could respond by taxing EU wood imports. GD Holz also contacted relevant German ministries to seek exclusion of timber trade from the dispute. German spruce sawfalling 44-50×150 mm prices rose 3.17% month-on-month in January 2026. European supply chains have adjusted since the 2022 loss of Russian and Belarusian timber, with Finland emerging as an enlarged supplier. US lumber futures traded at approximately 400–450 USD per 1000 board feet in early 2026. EU countries imported 24% more hardwood veneers from the US in 2025, with Germany receiving exports worth 18.9 million USD.

response:
**Headline Finding** — The most pressing EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) deadline for German timber companies is 30 December 2026 for large and medium operators.

Recent regulatory changes have postponed the main EUDR compliance deadline for large and medium-sized timber operators in Germany to 30 December 2026, according to Regulation (EU) 2025/2650 published in December 2025. This postponement was confirmed in multiple sources, with the revised regulation also introducing clarifications to definitions and due diligence obligations, while aiming to reduce burdens on smaller operators.

For micro and small enterprises, the compliance deadline has been extended to 30 June 2027. The amendments, which entered into force on 26 December 2025, provide all companies with an additional year to implement the new requirements. The core obligations for large operators—such as collecting supplier data and conducting risk assessments—remain unchanged despite the delay.

A further development to monitor is the mandated European Commission review of the regulation’s administrative burden, due by 30 April 2026. This review could potentially result in additional legislative changes, but as of Q1 2026, the 30 December 2026 deadline is the primary compliance date for most German timber companies.

30 December 2026 is the decisive EUDR compliance deadline now driving operational planning for large and medium German timber operators.
reference: The most pressing EUDR deadline for German timber companies is 30 December 2026 for large and medium operators under Regulation EU 2025/2650. Micro and small enterprises have until 30 June 2027 to comply. The European Commission must deliver a formal simplification review by 30 April 2026 which may result in further legislative changes. The amendments officially entered into force on 26 December 2025.

**Headline Finding** — Wood pellet demand in Germany in February and March 2026 is strong, but acute raw material scarcity is constraining sawmill byproduct availability and limiting pellet production growth.

Reporting from February 2026 confirms that pellet prices in Germany have risen sharply, driven by a combination of a cold spell and a surge in demand. Source 1 states that the situation is exacerbated by a pronounced shortage of raw wood, which has pushed roundwood prices to their highest level in 35 years at the start of 2026. This scarcity has led to "gähnend leere Läger" (completely empty warehouses) at many sawmills and disrupted raw wood supply, directly impacting the availability of all wood byproducts, including those used for pellet production.

Despite the strong demand for pellets, the supply of sawmill byproducts remains tight. Source 2, citing the Deutscher Energieholz- und Pellet-Verband (DEPV), notes that only about half of the annual sawmill byproduct volume is currently processed into pellets. The DEPV expects pellet production in 2026 to remain at a similar level to 2025, with over 4 million tonnes produced, due to a sluggish heating market and limited new installations of pellet stoves and boilers (35,000 units sold in 2025, a 14% increase). However, the report emphasizes that the potential for increased pellet production exists, but is not being realized under current market conditions.

Available price data from late February and early March 2026 (Source 3) further corroborates the tightness in both the pellet and sawmill residue markets, with no indication that byproduct volumes are sufficient to fully meet the heightened pellet demand. Instead, the acute raw material shortage is described as having "massive Auswirkungen" (massive impacts) on downstream wood utilization, including pellet production.

Pellet prices rising in February 2026, combined with sawmill byproduct scarcity and stagnant production volumes, confirm that current German pellet demand is outpacing the available supply of sawmill byproducts, limiting the sector's ability to absorb all byproduct volumes.
reference: Wood pellet demand in Germany is strong in February and March 2026 driven by a cold winter and heating demand surge. However acute raw material scarcity is constraining sawmill byproduct availability and limiting pellet production growth. Roundwood prices reached their highest level in 35 years in early 2026. The DEPV expects 2026 pellet production to remain similar to 2025 levels. Only approximately half of annual sawmill byproducts are currently being converted to pellets.

**Headline Finding** — An acute shortage of spruce and pine roundwood drove German softwood timber prices to new record highs between January and March 2026.

Sources confirm that the German softwood timber market experienced steep price increases in early 2026, with prices for spruce and pine roundwood reaching record levels by March. According to agrarheute (20 March 2026), the main driver was not increased demand but a noticeably tight supply, particularly for industrial softwood. Sawmills, facing reduced availability of sawmill by-products for pellet production, were forced to purchase more industrial wood on the open market, further pushing up prices.

According to industry reporting, the ongoing bark beetle crisis has not only reduced the proportion of usable spruce but also pushed sawmills to substitute pine where possible and to pay record prices for both high and lower quality spruce logs. Spruce B 2b+ at €130–135/Fm in Q1 2026 — a record price level that sources trace directly to bark beetle-driven reductions in usable spruce quality and supply.

**Headline Finding** — EUDR compliance has sharply increased documentation and traceability burdens for German timber importers, prompting calls for regulatory simplification and influencing sourcing strategies as of March 2026.

The European Union Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), scheduled for full implementation on December 30, 2026 under Regulation (EU) 2025/2650, imposes strict due diligence and traceability requirements on timber and other commodities entering the EU market. German timber importers and manufacturers, including furniture producers and paper mills, are now legally responsible for tracing their supply chains back to origin and submitting Due Diligence Statements (DDS) that confirm negligible risk of non-compliance. Even when exporters or certification bodies provide documentation, the legal responsibility remains with the German importer, significantly increasing administrative and documentation workloads.

A central challenge for German importers is the mandatory collection of detailed supplier data, including geolocation information for all harvest areas. The Gesamtverband Deutscher Holzhandel (GD Holz) has highlighted that the requirement to record geolocations for every harvest plot is particularly burdensome, especially in low-risk regions, and has called for the elimination of this obligation. Additionally, GD Holz notes that unclear legal definitions and inconsistent documentation requirements across EU member states have created further uncertainty, prompting demands for precise EU-wide guidelines and harmonized enforcement.

These heightened compliance burdens are already affecting sourcing patterns. Because navigating multi-tier supply chains and collecting the necessary data can take several months, German importers are increasingly prioritizing suppliers with robust traceability systems and are initiating managed outreach programs earlier in the procurement process. The complexity and time required to secure compliant documentation are leading some companies to reconsider sourcing from regions or suppliers unable to provide the required geolocation and legal proofs, effectively narrowing their supplier base.

GD Holz and other industry groups have responded by lobbying for regulatory simplification, including a one-year postponement of EUDR obligations and the removal of certain documentation requirements, to make compliance more practical for the sector. The EU Council's recent support for such measures, and Germany's own amendment proposals as of November 14, 2025, reflect ongoing efforts to reduce the administrative burden on the timber trade.

EUDR’s documentation and traceability requirements have directly increased the compliance workload for German timber importers, and this is already driving a shift toward suppliers with stronger data transparency and away from those unable to meet the new standards.
reference: EUDR compliance has significantly increased documentation and traceability burdens for German timber importers. Operators must implement due diligence systems tracing supply chains back to origin, capture geolocation data, and submit Due Diligence Statements. Approximately 60,000 to 80,000 companies globally are affected. The GD Holz has called for simplification including removal of geolocation requirements and reference number transfer obligations. The April 2026 EU Commission simplification review may provide further clarification.

**Headline Finding** — Salvage logging from storm and bark beetle damage has been significantly lower in recent years, directly contributing to record-high roundwood prices and acute supply shortages in Germany in early 2026.

Sources confirm that the volume of salvage wood from both storm damage and bark beetle infestations has been "much less" in recent periods compared to previous years. This decline in salvage logging is explicitly cited as a key factor behind the current scarcity of raw wood. Sawmills across Germany report "empty warehouses" and "stagnant" raw wood supply, with the Hauptverband der Deutschen Holzindustrie (HDH) warning of a rapidly deteriorating roundwood supply situation as of Q1 2026.

The direct market impact is clear: roundwood prices at the start of 2026 have reached their highest level in 35 years, with spruce and pine roundwood fetching record revenues for forest owners. Multiple sources from January to March 2026 describe the price increases as "steep" and "significant," driven by the acute raw material shortage. The shortage is so severe that even lower-quality, industrial, and energy wood grades are now benefiting from the price surge.

Industry associations, including the German Federal Association of the Wood Industry and the HDH, describe the situation as an "existential crisis" for sawmills and the broader timber industry if additional wood does not come to market soon. The Restoration Ordinance (WVO) is also mentioned as a factor affecting sustainable harvesting, but the immediate driver of the shortage is the reduced inflow of salvage wood from storms and bark beetle events.

Roundwood prices at their highest in 35 years in early 2026 — a direct result of sharply reduced salvage logging from storm and beetle damage constraining short-term supply.
reference: Salvage wood from bark beetle damage and storms has been significantly lower in recent years compared to prior periods directly contributing to record roundwood prices in early 2026. Roundwood prices reached their highest level in 35 years in early 2026. The reduced availability of salvage wood has reduced sawmill byproduct availability and tightened supply of pellet raw material. The HDH describes the situation as an extreme shortage and warns of escalation to an existential crisis for the sawmill industry.

**Headline Finding** — Major German engineered wood manufacturers are expanding production capacity in 2026 in direct response to rising demand for timber frame construction, with multiple large-scale CLT and glulam projects underway.

The Josef Schmelter GmbH in Lennestadt, North Rhine-Westphalia, has begun construction of a new cross-laminated timber (CLT, or Brettsperrholz) plant, scheduled to open in summer 2026. The investment totals approximately €20 million, and the company explicitly cites growing demand in project-based timber construction as the driver for this expansion. Schmelter aims to supply primarily the Central and Northern German markets, positioning the new facility to meet increased requirements for sustainable building materials.

Similarly, the Ziegler Group is undertaking a major expansion in Tirschenreuth, with plans for two engineered wood plants—each with an annual capacity of 100,000 m³—dedicated to both CLT and glulam (Brettschichtholz) production. The project, referred to as a "Gigafactory," will also include a prefabricated wall element line, a house construction line, and a large warehouse for finished components. This initiative is designed to address the surge in demand for engineered wood products driven by the growth of timber frame construction.

Industry-wide, the positive outlook for timber construction is reinforced by recent data showing a 6% year-on-year increase in housing permits in Germany, with 151,000 units approved and an 8% rise in construction orders up to July 2026. The German Säge- und Holzindustrie Bundesverband (DeSH) highlights the government's reactivation of the KfW-EH55 subsidy program for new builds as a significant policy measure supporting this trend, aiming to reduce the construction backlog and accelerate the delivery of urgently needed housing.

DACH+HOLZ International 2026, held in February in Cologne, has further spotlighted the sector’s adaptation to these trends, with a focus on new technologies, prefabrication, and efficiency improvements in timber construction trades. This reflects a broader industry shift toward scaling up engineered wood production and integrating advanced manufacturing solutions to meet the evolving demands of the German construction market.

Schmelter’s €20 million CLT plant and Ziegler’s dual 100,000 m³/year engineered wood factories are direct manufacturer responses to the surge in timber frame construction demand, with government policy and rising housing permits providing additional impetus for capacity expansion.
reference: German engineered wood manufacturers are expanding production capacity in 2026 in response to rising timber frame construction demand. Josef Schmelter GmbH in Lennestadt began construction of a new CLT plant scheduled to open in summer 2026 with an investment of approximately 20 million euros. The Ziegler Group is developing a large glulam and CLT facility in Tirschenreuth with two plants each targeting 100,000 cubic meters annual capacity. The German government reactivated the KfW EH55 subsidy programme in January 2026 supporting new timber construction.

response:
**Headline Finding** — Bark beetle damage has sharply reduced German spruce supply in early 2026, driving up prices and prompting sawmills to shift toward pine processing as an alternative domestic species.

Reporting from January and February 2026 confirms that German timber markets are experiencing extreme scarcity of spruce, with sawmills facing empty warehouses and acute raw material shortages. Source 1 states that prices for both spruce and pine are "higher than ever before" at the start of 2026, and that forest farmers are receiving record revenues for coniferous roundwood. The shortage is so severe that even lower-quality and industrial wood grades are benefiting from the supply squeeze.

The ecological driver of this scarcity is directly linked to bark beetle damage, as confirmed by Source 2, which details ongoing bark beetle-related forest damage in the Harz region as of March 2026. This environmental impact has reduced the availability of fresh spruce logs, compounding the supply crisis for sawmills.

In response to the spruce shortage, Source 3 reports that sawmills have begun shifting production capacity toward pine, where raw material availability is more favorable. This adjustment is a direct reaction to the price premiums and limited supply of spruce, with the pine-spruce species imbalance that characterized late 2025 showing signs of continuing into early 2026. Spruce sawfalling 44-50×150 mm in Germany edged up by 3.17% from December 2025 to January 2026, highlighting the price pressure on this species.

Spruce supply losses from bark beetle damage in early 2026 have led German timber buyers and sawmills to increase reliance on pine as an alternative domestic species, with no direct evidence yet of a significant shift toward imports.
reference: Bark beetle damage has sharply reduced German spruce supply in early 2026 driving up prices to record levels. Sawmills are shifting production capacity toward pine processing as an alternative domestic species where raw material availability is more favorable. Spruce prices edged up 3.17% in January 2026 in Germany while other grades held flat. Some mills also accept lower quality wood grades due to spruce scarcity. Import substitution was not explicitly confirmed in the retrieved sources.

**Headline Finding** — Roundwood prices in Germany continued to rise sharply through Q1 2026, with no evidence that the price impact of salvage logging has peaked or fully worked through the supply chain.

According to the German Timber Industry Association (HDH), the supply of raw timber deteriorated rapidly entering 2026, with available volumes of spruce and pine roundwood insufficient to meet sawmill demand. This supply tightness has led to exceptionally high timber prices in Q1 2026, with sources confirming that prices rose significantly again during this period. Sawmills are reportedly offering high prices to incentivize forest owners to increase supply, indicating ongoing pressure in the market.

Forest Machine Magazine, reporting on 22 January 2026, also confirms that German timber prices for spruce and pine reached new record highs, with sawmills paying forest owners sharply increased prices. This aligns with the broader industry view that the market remains in a state of tight supply and elevated prices, with no indication that the effects of salvage logging have fully dissipated.

No source provides evidence that the roundwood price impact of salvage logging has peaked by Q1 2026; instead, the persistent supply constraints and continued price increases suggest that the effects are still working through the supply chain.

Roundwood prices rose significantly again in Q1 2026 — a direct result of ongoing supply tightness that sources attribute to insufficient volumes reaching sawmills, indicating that the market impact of salvage logging has not yet peaked.
reference: Roundwood prices in Germany continued rising through Q1 2026 with no evidence of the salvage logging price impact peaking. Spruce and pine roundwood supply remained insufficient to meet sawmill demand throughout the quarter. The HDH reports raw timber supply deteriorated rapidly entering 2026. Sawmills fear inventories will be insufficiently stocked after the harvesting season. Current contracts with sawmills run through end of April 2026.

response:
**Headline Finding** — Acute timber shortages and high prices in early 2026 are directly linked by industry sources to the impact of carbon-related regulations, notably the Restoration Ordinance (WVO), on commercially available timber volumes in Germany.

The German timber market began 2026 with exceptionally high prices and a tight supply situation, particularly for spruce and pine roundwood. According to Forest Machine Magazine (17 March 2026) and Global Wood (16 March 2026), available volumes in many areas are insufficient to fully meet sawmills’ demand, resulting in significant price increases in the first quarter of 2026. Sawmills are reportedly short on supply, with contracts often running only through the end of April, and shipments moving quickly to meet urgent needs.

The Hauptverband der Deutschen Holzindustrie (HDH) has explicitly warned that the supply of raw timber has "rapidly deteriorated" and directly attributes this to the consequences of the Restoration Ordinance (WVO), a regulation tied to Germany’s climate and carbon goals. The WVO’s "prohibition on degradation" is cited as a key factor constraining sustainable forest management and reducing the volume of timber that can be harvested and brought to market. This link is confirmed in both agrarheute (11 March 2026) and Global Wood (16 March 2026), with the HDH cautioning that these regulatory measures are exacerbating the acute shortage of commercially available timber.

In addition to the WVO, the German federal government’s "Klimaschutzprogramm 2026" is referenced by Holz-Zentralblatt as a newly adopted policy package that includes climate-related forestry measures. While the specific mechanisms are not detailed in the sources, the timing and context suggest that such carbon incentive programs are part of the regulatory environment influencing timber supply in early 2026.

The direct attribution by the HDH and repeated warnings in industry reporting establish a clear causal link: carbon incentive programs and associated regulations, especially the WVO, are measurably reducing the volumes of commercially available timber in Germany as of Q1 2026, contributing to the current supply shortages and price spikes.

---
KEY_FACTS
["Restoration Ordinance (WVO) cited by HDH as reducing timber supply in March 2026", "Spruce and pine roundwood volumes insufficient for sawmills’ demand in Q1 2026", "Klimaschutzprogramm 2026 adopted by German federal government includes climate forestry measures"]
reference: The Restoration Ordinance (WVO) is cited by the HDH as a factor contributing to reduced commercially available timber volumes in Germany in early 2026. The prohibition on degradation under the WVO is identified as a specific concern for sustainable forest management. The German government adopted a climate protection programme in March 2026 including new impulses for forest conversion and climate adaptation. Carbon incentives are contributing to forest owners delaying harvests but this is not quantified in the retrieved sources.

**Headline Finding** — German sawmills are actively expanding engineered wood product capacity, with companies like Eugen Decker modernizing facilities to meet rising timber frame construction demand in early 2026.

Sources confirm that German sawmills are experiencing strong demand for wood products in Q1 2026, with roundwood prices—particularly for spruce—trading well above the €130 per Fm mark. This surge is attributed to both high demand and constrained supply, leading sawmills to accept lower quality logs and face significantly increased procurement costs. Despite these challenges, the stable and robust demand for wood products is supporting strong sales for the sector.

Eugen Decker, a major German sawmill and engineered wood producer, reports recent investments in new office buildings, a cross-cutting station, and modernization of their chip mill as of 2025. The company highlights its unique position as both a sawmill and a producer of laminated timber and cross-laminated timber (Brettsperrholz, CLT) at a single site, enabling efficient use of roundwood and supporting the shift toward engineered wood products. This operational model is presented as a strategic response to evolving market needs, including the growing demand for timber frame construction.

Industry documentation from Stora Enso further details the technical and market growth of engineered wood products such as CLT across Europe, noting their increasing use in large-scale construction projects due to prefabrication advantages and structural versatility. While this source does not provide German-specific capacity figures, it supports the broader trend of rising engineered wood product adoption in response to construction sector demand.

Spruce roundwood prices exceeding €130 per Fm in Q1 2026, combined with Eugen Decker’s 2025 investments in CLT and laminated timber production, demonstrate that German sawmills are shifting capacity toward engineered wood products as a direct response to increased timber frame construction demand.
reference: German sawmills are expanding engineered wood product capacity in Q1 2026. Eugen Decker modernized its facility including a new office building capping station and chip plant. Josef Schmelter GmbH began building a new CLT plant in Lennestadt NRW for summer 2026 opening. Roundwood prices for spruce B 2b+ exceeded 130 euros per cubic meter and sawmills are accepting lower quality grades due to scarcity.

German softwood timber prices in Q1 2026 are significantly higher than in Q1 2025, driven by acute supply shortages and reduced harvest volumes.

According to Forest Machine Magazine (17 March 2026), the German timber market began 2026 with exceptionally high prices for softwood roundwood, particularly spruce and pine. The supply situation remains tight, with available volumes in many areas insufficient to fully meet sawmill demand. This led to significant price increases in the first quarter of 2026, with additional premiums sometimes paid for fresh industrial wood. The cold and snowy winter accelerated inventory reduction and sharply increased demand for energy wood.

Globalwood.org (21 January 2026) confirms that prices for spruce and pine in Germany are higher than ever before in 2026, with the timber industry raising prices significantly compared to the previous year. The German Federal Association of the Wood Industry describes the current situation as a “raw material shortage.” Sawmills report empty warehouses due to very limited deliveries of fresh logs. The extreme shortage is attributed to a very low supply of damaged wood, which, according to the Bavarian State Institute for Forestry and Forest Management, typically accounts for about half of the timber harvested and supplied to the market.

EUWID (26 February 2026) reports that the Eberbacher Submission, a key German timber auction held on 18 February 2026, offered 1,021 fm of softwood for sale, a 15% reduction from the previous year’s 1,196 fm. This decline was mainly due to the halved volume from state forests, further constraining supply.

The spread between Q1 2026 and Q1 2025 softwood timber prices is described as “significant” and “higher than ever before,” with a 15% year-on-year reduction in auctioned volumes and acute raw material shortages as the primary drivers.
reference: German softwood timber prices in Q1 2026 are significantly higher than in Q1 2025. Spruce and pine roundwood prices reached record levels in Q1 2026. Forest owners are receiving approximately 25 to 30 euros more per cubic meter than the previous year. At the Eberbacher submission in February 2026 the volume of softwood offered fell 15% compared to 2025 due to reduced state forest supply. The primary driver of the year-on-year increase is supply shortage rather than increased demand.

**Headline Finding** — In February 2026, wood pellet prices in Germany continued to rise but maintained a price advantage of roughly 25% over heating oil, supporting strong biomass heating demand during a cold winter.

According to the German Pellet Institute (DEPI), the average price for a tonne of wood pellets in January 2026 was €405.33 for a 6-tonne purchase, with the cost per kilowatt-hour of pellet energy at 8.11 euro cents. While February 2026 saw further increases in pellet prices, sources confirm that pellets remained more affordable than heating oil, with the long-term price advantage over heating oil holding at approximately 25%. This relative price gap has been consistent in recent years, despite the upward movement in pellet prices.

Weekly heating oil prices in Germany, as reported by agrarheute.com, provide the basis for this comparison, though the exact February 2026 heating oil price is not specified in the available sources. Nonetheless, the persistent price advantage for pellets is explicitly stated by DEPI and corroborated by market reporting, even as both energy sources experienced price increases during the winter.

The cold and snowy winter of early 2026 led to sharply increased demand for energy wood, including pellets, as households and businesses sought to maintain heating. Forest Machine Magazine notes that this intense heating season accelerated inventory reduction and further boosted demand for high-quality firewood and energy wood. Despite tight timber markets and rising costs for all wood assortments, the continued price advantage of pellets over heating oil has supported robust biomass heating demand.

Wood pellets at €405.33/tonne in January 2026 — with a sustained 25% price advantage over heating oil — directly contributed to strong biomass heating demand during the cold winter months.
reference: Wood pellets in Germany averaged 405.33 euros per tonne for a 6-tonne purchase in January 2026 with the cost per kilowatt-hour at 8.11 euro cents. Wood pellets maintain approximately a 25% price advantage over heating oil and approximately 30% over natural gas. Pellet prices continued rising in February 2026 due to the cold winter and raw material scarcity. Strong biomass heating demand is confirmed by rising firewood and pellet prices throughout February and March 2026.

response:
**Headline Finding** — German sawmill gas costs surged by 50% in Q1 2026 compared to Q1 2025, while electricity costs fell by around 7%, with sharply higher gas prices and raw material shortages squeezing production profitability despite government relief measures.

According to agrarheute and Green Planet Energy, European gas prices in Germany rose by 50% year-on-year to reach €44/MWh in early March 2026, with a further spike to over €54/MWh on March 3, 2026, driven by the Iran conflict and low storage levels. This marks the highest gas price in three years, with the increase occurring rapidly—from €32/MWh on February 27 to over €54/MWh by March 3, a nearly 70% jump within days. The removal of the Gasspeicherumlage (gas storage levy) on January 1, 2026, which previously accounted for 0.34 cents/kWh (about 2.4% of the gas price), provided some relief, but was largely offset by a rise in gas network charges of up to 12% across most regions.

For electricity, the German government implemented a €6.5 billion subsidy to reduce grid fees in 2026, resulting in an average saving of 1.52 cents/kWh for consumers and an overall decrease in household electricity prices by about 7%. However, sources note that electricity suppliers are not required to pass on the full savings, and regional differences persist. The federal government estimates that combined electricity and gas relief measures will save the average consumer about €160 in 2026 (based on 3,500 kWh electricity and 20,000 kWh gas annual consumption).

Despite these policy interventions, sawmills face mounting profitability challenges. Bauernzeitung reports that raw material shortages and increased demand have driven up spruce (Fichte B 2b+) and pine prices by €5–10 per cubic meter at the start of 2026, with some locations seeing €25–30 higher prices than the previous year. Sawmills are experiencing depleted inventories and higher procurement costs, compounding the impact of surging gas prices on overall production margins.

Gas prices rising by 50% year-on-year to €44/MWh in Q1 2026, coupled with raw material cost inflation, have directly eroded sawmill profitability, with only partial offset from a 7% reduction in electricity costs due to government subsidies.
reference: German gas prices rose approximately 50% year-on-year reaching 44 euros per MWh in early March 2026 and spiking to over 54 euros per MWh on 3 March 2026 due to the Iran conflict. Electricity costs for households fell approximately 7% in 2026 due to reduced network charges subsidized by the government at 6.5 billion euros annually. The electricity tax for industry was permanently reduced to the EU minimum from 2026. Despite energy cost relief measures sawmill production profitability was under pressure from record roundwood prices.

response:
**Headline Finding** — Spruce roundwood prices in Germany in March 2026 are higher than in January 2026, driven by acute supply shortages and strong sawmill demand.

According to multiple sources, the German timber market began 2026 with exceptionally high prices for spruce roundwood. The German Timber Industry Association (HDH) reports that the supply of raw timber, particularly spruce, has deteriorated rapidly, with available volumes insufficient to meet sawmills’ needs. This tight supply situation has led to significant price increases within the first quarter of 2026.

For March 2026, market participants report that spruce fixed-length B/C grade and B grade long timber in the 2b+ thickness class is being paid at around €130–€133 per cubic meter, with some cases reaching as high as €135 per cubic meter. For B/C grades in the 2b+ class, prices are currently around €96–€100 per cubic meter. Beetle-damaged spruce timber is typically discounted by €20 per cubic meter from these levels.

Sources confirm that prices rose significantly again in the first quarter of 2026, with sawmills attempting to use high prices to incentivize forest owners to increase supply. The Restoration Ordinance (WVO) and its prohibition on degradation are also cited as factors potentially impacting sustainable forest management and further constraining supply.

The spread between March and January 2026 spruce roundwood prices is an increase of several euros per cubic meter, with March prices at €130–€135/Fm for B/C and B grade 2b+, driven by worsening supply shortages and robust sawmill demand.
reference: Spruce roundwood prices in Germany in March 2026 are higher than in January 2026. Prices rose consistently throughout Q1 2026 driven by acute supply shortage. Spruce B 2b+ ranged from 125 to over 145 euros per cubic meter in March 2026 with a clear north-south price gap with North Rhine-Westphalia approaching 150 euros. In January spruce B 2b+ averaged 129 to 132 euros per cubic meter. The supply situation remained tight throughout the quarter with sawmill inventories depleted.
