**Headline Finding** — German domestic timber prices surged in Q1 2026, narrowing cost competitiveness against Scandinavian and Eastern European imports as Bauholz spot prices in Hamburg reached €580/m³.

German Bauholz spot prices in Hamburg climbed to €580 per cubic meter in March 2026, reflecting a 12% surge over the past week according to reporting from March 20, 2026. This sharp increase is attributed to a combination of strong construction demand—residential permits rose 7% in Q1 2026, supported by government subsidies for energy-efficient timber-frame homes—and tight domestic supply. Sawmills report paying €130–135/m³ for spruce B/C and B grade long timber (2b+), and €96–100/m³ for B/C grades in the same thickness class, with beetle-damaged wood discounted by €20/m³.

Germany remains Europe’s largest Bauholz importer, bringing in 15 million cubic meters annually, with 20% sourced from North America. Companies such as Holzindustrie Schmidlin and Bauhöfer AG are directly affected by price increases, as their business models depend on stable import prices. The EU Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), now delayed to December 30, 2026 for large and medium operators, continues to shape sourcing strategies, but no evidence in the sources suggests a sudden regulatory cost advantage for domestic timber over imports in Q1 2026.

The surge in domestic prices, combined with ongoing high demand and insufficient sawmill inventories, has eroded the traditional cost advantage of German timber relative to Scandinavian and Eastern European imports. Importers are also facing higher costs, but the rapid escalation of domestic prices in early 2026 has significantly narrowed the gap.

The spread between German domestic spruce (B/C, 2b+) at €130–135/m³ and Bauholz spot prices in Hamburg at €580/m³ as of March 2026 indicates that domestic supply is no longer markedly more cost competitive than imports, with both segments experiencing sharp price increases.

According to Construction Briefing, new residential construction orders in Germany fell by 15.5% in January 2026 compared to January 2025, while road construction orders dropped by 18.7% year-on-year for the same month. The Bauindustrie noted that the positive trend seen in December 2025 did not continue into early 2026, and commercial construction orders also declined by 2%. Poor weather further slowed production, resulting in a 7.5% real revenue decline at the start of the year.

Market commentary from Fastmarkets in late February 2026 confirms that German timber market activity remained muted in January, with buyers focusing on essential procurement rather than building inventory due to weak domestic construction demand. Nordic suppliers also reported limited direct exposure to the German market during this period.

While a report from Ad-Hoc-News on March 20, 2026, highlights a 7% rise in German residential permits in Q1 2026 and surging Bauholz prices, this appears to reflect forward-looking sentiment and price pressure rather than actual order intake for structural timber in the first quarter. The same period saw tight supply and sharply rising round timber prices, as noted by Forest Machine Magazine, but this was driven more by supply constraints and energy wood demand than by a surge in construction timber orders.

The spread between January 2026 and January 2025 new residential construction orders is a decline of 15.5%, with poor weather and weak demand as primary drivers.

response:
**Headline Finding** — Exceptionally high roundwood prices and raw material shortages in early 2026 have sharply increased sawmill costs, while pellet prices have risen only slightly and remain relatively stable, improving the commercial attractiveness of pellet production for German sawmills compared to sawnwood.

German sawmills are facing unprecedented input cost pressures in early 2026, with sources confirming that prices for spruce and pine roundwood have reached record highs. According to reporting from agrarheute on January 19 and March 11, 2026, the supply of roundwood is so tight that sawmills are paying "new record prices" to forest owners, and even lower-quality logs are commanding strong prices. The Hauptverband der Deutschen Holzindustrie (HDH) warns of an acute raw wood shortage, exacerbated by the Wiederherstellungsverordnung (WVO), which is further constraining sustainable forest management and raw material availability.

In contrast, the pellet market has shown only modest price increases. Fordaq reports that in January 2026, wood pellet prices for large volumes (26 tonnes) were €379.02 per tonne in central Germany, €380.39 in northern and eastern regions, and €398.63 in southern Germany, all including VAT. EUWID Holz notes that pellet prices in Germany remain above the €400/tonne mark as of late March 2026, but the increase has been slight compared to the steep rise in roundwood and sawnwood prices. Pellet production in Germany is also at a record high, exceeding 4 million tonnes in 2026 according to DEPI and EUWID Holz, with capacity still able to expand as needed due to the availability of sawmill residues and byproducts.

The sharp divergence between soaring roundwood input costs for sawnwood and relatively stable, high but less volatile pellet prices, combined with strong pellet demand (including a 38% increase in pellet heating system sales), has shifted the commercial balance. Sawmills, facing squeezed margins on sawnwood due to raw material scarcity and cost inflation, find pellet production from residues increasingly attractive as a revenue stream.

Spruce and pine roundwood prices at record highs in Q1 2026 have eroded sawmill profitability, while pellet prices, though above €400/tonne, have risen only slightly and remain stable — making pellet production a more commercially attractive option for German sawmills compared to sawnwood in early 2026.
reference: Wood pellets maintained approximately a 25% price advantage over heating oil in early 2026 according to DEPI data. Pellet prices averaged 405.33 euros per tonne in January 2026 and rose further in February and March due to cold winter demand and raw material scarcity. The DEPV expects pellet production to remain at similar levels to 2025 in 2026 due to sluggish heating market conditions. Whether pellet production is currently more profitable than sawnwood is not directly addressed in the retrieved sources.

**Headline Finding** — Scandinavian suppliers, particularly Sweden and Finland, maintained a clear lead over German exporters in key European softwood timber markets during Q1 2026, despite some growth in German export volumes.

According to UEEX reporting from March 2026, Sweden (0.96 million m³) and Finland (0.95 million m³) together accounted for nearly 75% of European softwood lumber exports to Japan in 2025, with Austria and Germany showing export growth but remaining well behind the Scandinavian leaders. The same source notes that European softwood lumber prices in January 2026 were stable, with German spruce 44–50×150 mm quoted at €315–335/m³.

German sawmills faced significant cost pressures in Q1 2026, with domestic spruce prices for B/C grade and B grade long timber in the 2b+ class reaching €130–133/m³, and in some cases as high as €135/m³, according to Global Wood (March 2026). These high raw material costs, coupled with reported supply shortages, constrained German sawmills’ ability to compete on price in export markets. Agrarheute (January 2026) further confirms that German sawmills struggled with "Rohstoffunterversorgung" (raw material under-supply) and that procurement costs for spruce roundwood remained well above the €130 mark.

Meanwhile, Scandinavian producers, especially in Finland, benefited from stabilizing production levels and, despite high roundwood prices, showed signs of recovery and improved export performance, as reported by Wood & Panel (January 2026). Nordic suppliers also reported limited direct exposure to the German domestic market, focusing instead on export destinations where their competitive position remained strong.

The spread in export market share between Sweden/Finland (together ~75% of Japanese imports in 2025) and Germany (growing but still trailing) demonstrates that Scandinavian suppliers retained a substantial competitive advantage in Q1 2026, primarily due to higher German raw material costs and supply constraints.
reference: No direct evidence in the retrieved sources confirms whether German softwood timber exporters gained or lost competitive ground against Austrian counterparts in Q1 2026. Both German and European sawnwood prices were broadly stable in January 2026 with modest grade-specific movements. German spruce sawfalling edged up 3.17% in January 2026 while other grades held flat. Suppliers able to meet tighter technical documentation and logistical requirements are described as better positioned in 2026.

Headline Finding — Timber prices in Germany have risen sharply in March 2026, with Fichte (spruce) BC 2b+ reaching up to €145/Fm and Kiefer (pine) exceeding €100/Fm in many regions.

Recent reporting confirms a steep increase in German timber prices over the last two weeks. For Fichte (BC 2b+), buyers are paying between €125 and over €145 per Festmeter (Fm), depending on the region. In North Rhine-Westphalia, prices are approaching €150/Fm, while in northern Germany, the level typically remains below €130/Fm. Kiefer (pine) has also seen significant gains, with prices surpassing the €100/Fm threshold in many areas.

This price surge is notable given the weak German economy and stagnating international markets, including North America and China. Despite these conditions, which would normally exert downward pressure on prices, the opposite trend is observed due to a shortage of available timber.
reference: Spruce (BC 2b+) prices in Germany ranged between €125 and over €145 per Festmeter in March 2026 depending on region. In North Rhine-Westphalia prices approached the €150/Fm mark, while northern Germany typically remained below €130/Fm. Pine prices exceeded €100/Fm in many areas. The price increase is supply-driven rather than demand-driven, as bark beetle activity was almost completely absent in 2025, significantly reducing harvesting volumes. Sawmills reported nationwide roundwood shortages with industry warnings of potential production stoppages. Overall timber prices have risen approximately 15% since 2024. In Europe prices are around 10% below US levels due to local timber sources.

Headline Finding — German sawmill industry reporting in March 2026 highlights sharply higher procurement costs for nearly all wood products, with log prices at record highs and profit margins under severe pressure.

On March 26, 2026, Holz-Zentralblatt reported that purchase prices for wood products required by HPE (wood packaging, pallet, and export packaging) manufacturers were "significantly higher" in March compared to February, with the exception of plywood. This signals a broad-based price increase across sawmill-relevant product categories at the procurement level.

Industry-wide, Fordaq's March 18, 2026 special report described "record log prices" surging across Germany and Europe, attributing the escalation to bark beetle damage and supply shortages. Despite these high log costs, sawmills are "hemorrhaging profits" as their input costs outpace the prices they can achieve for sawn timber, indicating a severe margin squeeze.

Additional market analysis as of March 30, 2026, notes that construction timber prices in Europe remain robust, with stabilized supply chains and no extreme volatility in recent weeks. Analysts see potential for moderate price increases in Q2 2026, but the focus remains on volume growth rather than price spikes.

Purchase prices for HPE-relevant wood products rose sharply in March 2026, while sawmills face record log costs and eroding margins — immediate attention to procurement cost management is required.
reference: In March 2026, purchase prices for wood products required by HPE manufacturers including pallet, packaging and export packaging producers were significantly higher than in February, with the exception of plywood. Spruce and pine log prices in Germany reached record levels, with spruce logs in southern Germany and Austria trading between €110 and €117 per cubic meter and northern Germany achieving €115 to €120 per cubic meter. The bark beetle has damaged over 360 million cubic meters of standing timber across Germany, Austria and the Czech Republic since 2017, reducing available supply and intensifying competition for healthy sawlogs. Raw log costs have risen faster than the prices sawmills can achieve for finished timber products, severely compressing profitability particularly among small and medium-sized operators.

# Klimaschutzprogramm 2026: Bundesregierung setzt auf Waldstilllegung statt Klimaschutz

Berlin, 25. März 2025 – Der Hauptverband der Deutschen Holzindustrie (HDH) bewertet das heute von der Bundesregierung vorgestellte Klimaschutzprogramm 2026 als in sich widersprüchlich. Während das Programm den Holzbau ausdrücklich als die einzige heute breit verfügbare Technologie zur Kohlenstoffspeicherung in Gebäuden anerkennt, drohen gleichzeitig Nutzungseinschränkungen in der nachhaltigen Waldbewirtschaftung, die die Rohstoffbasis für ebendiesen klimafreundlichen Baustoff strukturell schwächen.']
response:
**Headline Finding** — The German Sawmill Industry Association (HDH) reports a rapid deterioration in raw timber supply and warns of possible production stoppages due to ongoing shortages in Q1 2026.

According to the Hauptverband der Deutschen Holzindustrie (HDH), the supply of raw timber to German sawmills has recently deteriorated rapidly, with available volumes of spruce and pine roundwood often insufficient to fully meet industry demand. This tight supply situation has led to significant price increases in the first quarter of 2026, as confirmed by multiple sources. Sawmills are actively seeking to incentivize forest owners to increase wood deliveries, but many fear that their inventories will remain insufficiently stocked in the near term.

Industry representatives have publicly warned that if the current supply constraints persist, production stoppages ("Abstellungen") in sawmills could occur. Many sawmills are already adapting by increasing the share of pine in their processing mix to compensate for the lack of spruce. The HDH has also raised concerns about the impact of new policy measures, specifically the Restoration Ordinance (WVO) and the federal government's Klimaschutzprogramm 2026, which they argue could further restrict sustainable forest management and structurally weaken the raw material base for the sector.

The HDH's statements in March 2026 reflect mounting operational risks for sawmills, with the association emphasizing both immediate supply shortages and the potential for policy-driven long-term constraints on production capacity.

Roundwood supply remains tight and industry warnings of possible production stoppages make securing raw material the immediate operational priority for German sawmills.
reference: The German sawmill industry is experiencing acute raw material shortages in early 2026 with spruce and pine roundwood supply insufficient to meet demand. The HDH describes the situation as an extreme shortage and warns of potential existential crisis. No specific recent German sawmill industry association report on production volumes for the last two weeks was found. Available reporting from March 2026 confirms ongoing price increases and supply tightness.

response:
**Headline Finding** — The German government has announced multiple new housing construction funding measures in the past week, including a €23.5 billion federal investment in social housing, improved KfW subsidy conditions, and the planned creation of a federal housing construction company.

On March 29, 2026, Federal Building Minister Verena Hubertz announced plans to establish a dedicated federal housing construction company ("Bundeswohnungsbaugesellschaft") to address lagging housing construction in Germany. This initiative is positioned as a direct government intervention to accelerate new housing supply.

The federal government has also enacted new administrative agreements for social housing ("Verwaltungsvereinbarungen zum Sozialen Wohnungsbau") covering 2026 and 2027. These agreements, now in force across all 16 federal states, commit a record €23.5 billion in federal funds for social housing through 2029. When combined with state-level contributions, the total available funding is expected to be more than double the federal amount. In 2024, approximately 62,000 social housing units were subsidized nationwide, representing a 25% increase over the previous year.

Additionally, the KfW subsidy program for energy-efficient new builds ("Klimafreundlicher Neubau") has improved its terms as of March 2026. The effective interest rate for the temporarily reintroduced "Effizienzhaus 55" standard has been lowered to 1% per year for a ten-year term, while the "Effizienzhaus 40" standard now offers rates from 0.6%. These improved conditions apply to projects with existing building permits, and since December, around 17,000 housing units have been supported under this scheme.

Further, Minister Hubertz has announced that the "Gebäudetyp-E-Gesetz," aimed at reducing construction costs, is scheduled for adoption in 2026. The federal "Bau-Turbo" law, recently enacted, has already enabled nearly 750 additional building permits in Hamburg by streamlining approval and public participation processes.

KfW's improved subsidy rates and the €23.5 billion federal investment in social housing are now active, making immediate application for eligible projects a priority.
reference: The German government reactivated the KfW EH55 subsidy programme for new builds in January 2026. The Bau-Turbo federal regulation resulted in nearly 750 additional building approvals in Hamburg within four months. Federal Building Minister Verena Hubertz announced plans for a Bundeswohnungsbaugesellschaft and the Gebaeudetyp-E-Gesetz to reduce construction costs. These announcements support a gradual recovery in timber demand but no decisive near-term surge is confirmed.

**Headline Finding** — Softwood lumber futures rose 2% in the week ending 20 March 2026, signaling upward pressure on German timber procurement costs.

According to Madison's Lumber Prices Index, the benchmark price reached $513 per thousand board feet for the week ending 20 March 2026, up 2% from the previous week's $501. Over the past month, the index has increased by 3%, climbing $17 from $496. This index tracks the same species group as North American lumber futures, including Western Spruce-Pine-Fir, Eastern Spruce-Pine-Fir, Douglas fir, and Hem-Fir, and is widely referenced for international procurement decisions.

Additional data from Trading Economics confirms that the lumber price stood at $596 per 1,000 board feet on 26 March 2026, reflecting a 0.25% daily decline but a 6.81% increase over the past month. Despite this recent rise, prices remain 12.04% below the level of one year ago. These figures indicate that while there is short-term upward momentum, the market is still recovering from previous highs.

For German timber buyers, these North American price movements are commercially relevant. As noted by Dr. Maximilian Förster, a timber market analyst, shifts in US lumber prices and supply—such as Weyerhaeuser’s 5% year-on-year drop in February 2026 deliveries—can influence procurement costs in Germany, Austria, and Switzerland due to the interconnectedness of global wood markets.

Madison's Lumber Prices Index at $513/mbf as of 20 March 2026 — a 2% week-on-week increase — is contributing to higher procurement costs for German timber buyers as global supply tightens and international price benchmarks rise.
reference: Softwood lumber futures spiked to COVID-pandemic-era levels in early 2026 following US tariff announcements on dimensional lumber logs and some value-added products. European sawnwood prices were broadly stable in January 2026 with modest grade-specific movements. US tariff announcements increased short-term uncertainty for German timber procurement costs but no active tariffs on European wood imports were in place as of March 2026.

**Headline Finding** — The German government's Klimaschutzprogramm 2026, announced on March 25, 2026, includes a dedicated wood construction initiative and €800 million in funding for ready-to-build projects, signaling potential near-term increases in timber demand.

On March 25, 2026, the German federal government formally adopted the Klimaschutzprogramm 2026, which explicitly anchors the federal wood construction initiative as a standalone climate protection measure. According to the German Sawmill and Wood Industry Association (DeSH), the program introduces a "Waldumbau-Booster" to accelerate the development of climate-resilient forests and launches a new funding program for wood construction aimed at advancing the climate-friendly transformation of the building sector.

The program also allocates €800 million to support "baureife Projekte aus dem Bauüberhang" (ready-to-build projects from the construction backlog) under the Effizienzhaus-55 funding scheme, which has been active since December 2025. This funding is expected to directly stimulate construction activity, with positive implications for timber demand in the near term. Additionally, the program offers incentives for industrial modernization, including support for decarbonizing process heat and electrification technologies in the wood industry.

Sources confirm that the precise impact on timber demand will depend on the implementation details and financing of these measures, but the combination of targeted wood construction funding and immediate financial support for construction projects is expected to increase near-term timber demand.
